Notice Of Motion And Motion To Approve The Paga Settlement Pursuant To Labor Code Sec. 2699(L)(2)
Off Calendar Feb-20-2020 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances.

RULING: There being no appearance or objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on PLAINTIFF LEAH UNDERWOOD's Motion To Approve The Paga Settlement Pursuant To Labor Code Sec. 2699(L)(2) as follows:

Plaintiff Leah Underwood's motion to approve PAGA settlement is granted. Plaintiff seeks court approval pursuant to Labor Code section 2699(g) of a settlement providing for resolution of her claim under PAGA in exchange for payment of $2,500 to the LWDA. The confidential settlement agreement provides for a release of plaintiff's claims only.

The court notes that the parties' proposed settlement is anomalous, because there is no such thing as an "individual" PAGA claim. The Private Attorneys General Act of 2004 authorizes an aggrieved employee, acting as a private attorney general, to recover civil penalties for Labor Code violations "on behalf of himself or herself and other current or former employees against whom one or more of the alleged violations was committed." (Lab. Code 2699(g)(1) (emphasis added).) A PAGA action "is a representative action on behalf of the state." (Iskanian v. CLS Transp. Los Angeles, LLC (2014) 348, 387; see also Arias v. Superior Court (2009) 46 Cal.4th 969, 986.) "A plaintiff asserting a PAGA claim may not bring the claim simply on his or her own behalf but must bring it as a representative action and include 'other current or former employees.'" (Reyes v. Macy's, Inc. (2009) 202 Cal.App.4th 1119, 1123; see also Kahn v. Dunn-Edwards Corp. (2018) 19 Cal.App.5th 804, 810 n.1 ["[A] PAGA action is only a representative action. 'Permitting pursuit of only individual penalties appears inconsistent with PAGA's objectives.'" (quoting Tanguilig v. Bloomingdales, Inc. (2016) 5 Cal.App.5th 665, 676.))

In view of this authority, the court normally would deny approval of any proposed settlement, such as this one, that seeks to resolve PAGA claims and provides for penalties solely on behalf of an individual plaintiff, and does not provide for relief to any other aggrieved employee. On the record presented here, however, plaintiff shows that there are only two other employees who could have asserted similar claims within the statutory period, and that both of those employees resolved their claims with defendant by way of written settlement agreements. Solely in light of those circumstances, and limited to the facts of this case, the motion is granted.

The Court signed the proposed order submitted by email.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Not reported.
